title: Restrict outgoing network access
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

I am trying to block internet access from a container i.e.

- Request coming inside the container should work. ( World -> container )
- Request from container to outside world should not work. (Container -> World) ❌ 

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. I tried `--disable-dns` and `--internal`



**Describe the results you received:**

- Requests to domains blocked
- Requests to IP address still working. ❌ 

**Describe the results you expected:**

- Requests to domains blocked
- Requests to IP address blocked.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
4.2.1
```



**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Running on MacOS

comments.body:
Even tried with `--subnet 10.1.1.0/24` still was able to ping a server

```bash
$ ping 4..2.2.2 -c 1
> PING 4.2.2.2 (4.2.2.2): 56 data bytes
```

--disable-dns is just to disable the podman dns server which resolves other container names. It has nothing to do with what you want.

--internal only allows connections within the same network, if you are able to connect to ips outside of the network it is a bug.


It is not possible to only allow incoming and not outgoing, for such cases you have to manually configure your firewall. 

Also please provide the full info of information as requested in the issue template, we need at least `podman info`.

@Shubham-Kumar-2000 if your server software supports it, you could use _socket activation_ to block outgoing traffic and only allow incoming traffic that is connecting to your service. When using socket activation it's possible to run the container with `--network none` and still have the service serve the internet.
See https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/socket-activation-podman
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/socket_activation.md#socket-activation-of-containers
_Disclaimer:_ The technique works when running Podman on a Linux server but I guess it  would be difficult to get it to work when running Podman on macOS. (I haven't seen any examples of that)

Hi I want to understand that if I use `--internal` network.
And I `EXPOSE 3000` port from `Dockerfile`.

Then will I be able to hit the container service using `http://localhost:3000`

@eriksjolund

@Shubham-Kumar-2000 If you believe you've found a Podman bug, please provide a full reproducer with for instance a copy-paste of the shell session so that it's possible to see the exact commands that you have run. Specify which of those commands that gave a surprising result. In the new issue template there are some information fields that you've haven't filled out. 

**Output of `podman info`:**
**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman` or `brew info podman`):**

Sorry, I was drifting away from the topic of this GitHub issue myself when I brought up the technology _socket activation_ although it was not mentioned in this GitHub issue.

For question about how Podman is expected to work I think this is a good place to ask 
https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions
No I guess its me who is confused a bit.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I think the conclusion to this was no issue, so closing.  Reopen if I am mistaken.
